title: Hoeller Lab
text:
How does the brain turn the flat, ever-shifting images on the retina into a stable sense of depth and 3D structure—above all as we move through the world? We combine mathematical theory, computational modeling, and close collaboration with experimentalists to find out, linking large-scale neural datasets with artificial neural networks. Our goal is twofold: to uncover the computational principles unique to biological vision, and to build artificial architectures that capture them.
Our research Join the lab

title: Hoeller Lab
text:
News

January 2027 — Lab starts at the University of Notre Dame!

title: Join us
text:
New tools in neuroscience are producing data at a scale and quality we have never had before, and with it the chance to answer long-standing questions about how the brain computes. It is a good time for quantitative scientists to move into biology. We welcome people across disciplines who want to bring quantitative rigor to these questions.
We are recruiting at every level and offer a collaborative, supportive environment:

Postdocs
PhD students
Undergraduates (research assistants; we ask for a six-month minimum so there is time to train properly)

To apply, email me with your CV and a short description of your research interests and background—quantitative coursework, coding experience, or previous research. Postdoctoral candidates should also include contact details for two or three references. Prospective graduate students must apply through the university’s graduate program, but are encouraged to reach out first to discuss potential projects.

title: Contact
text:
Hoeller Lab Judith Hoeller Department of Physics University of Notre Dame
Email: jhoeller@nd.edu

title: Research
text: We study how visual systems—biological and artificial—represent a moving, three-dimensional world. Our projects span the theory of neural population codes, geometry-constrained deep learning, and connectome-based models of real circuits.

title: Research
section: The Neural Geometry of 3D Vision and Depth Cues
text:
The Neural Geometry of 3D Vision and Depth Cues
Perceiving depth feels effortless, yet the brain must compute distance and 3D structure from the 2D light entering a single eye. Movement makes this harder: as an animal travels through the world, its visual input undergoes continuous geometric transformations. We study how neuronal populations process these transformations to support both stable object recognition and accurate depth perception.

title: Research
text:
Mechanistic Models of Motion Parallax
Mechanistic understanding requires knowing how individual neurons wire together into functional circuits. Complete connectomes—such as the full wiring diagram of the fruit fly brain—now let us trace how sensory input becomes a navigational command. We investigate how the visual system computes relative depth from motion parallax by analyzing integration of motion signals across different neuronal populations.

title: Research
section: Geometric Deep Learning for Biological and Artificial Vision
text:
Geometric Deep Learning for Biological and Artificial Vision
Convolutional networks achieve remarkable performance on visual tasks, yet they process images unlike any biological brain and often remain “black boxes.” We push artificial networks closer to biological reality by building in mathematical and biological constraints. Using geometric deep learning, our networks share weights across channels to mimic the equivariant population codes found in real brains.

title: People
text: We are recruiting at every level—see Join us if you’d like to work with us.

title: People
text:
Current members





Judith Hoeller


Principal Investigator


Judith is a theoretical and computational scientist who uses her background in mathematics and physics to study how the brain processes visual information. She earned her degrees at ETH Zurich, completed a PhD in theoretical condensed-matter physics at Yale, and moved into systems neuroscience as a postdoc at HHMI’s Janelia Research Campus. Outside the lab she is usually outdoors—hiking, exploring, or swimming in lakes.
